qEnglish: Hina ..... in this organization for 4 years.
explanationEnglish: Present Perfect Continuous tense is used for an action started in the past and continuing in the present with a time duration.
subject: English

qEnglish: He knew that Abhinav ..... his watch.
explanationEnglish: Past Perfect tense is used for an action completed before another past action.
subject: English

qEnglish: If she were an actress, she ..... to you.
explanationEnglish: In a second conditional sentence (imaginary situation), 'would + base verb' is used in the main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: It was right time, we.....our preparation for the event.
explanationEnglish: After expressions like 'It is/was high time' or 'It is/was right time', the simple past tense (V2) is used.
subject: English

qEnglish: Pragati found that evaporated water...........clouds.
explanationEnglish: Scientific facts or general truths are expressed in the Simple Present tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Dipti says that she...........to see her family next week.
explanationEnglish: Future intent or scheduled action is expressed using the Future Simple tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Our constitution...........us the right to vote.
explanationEnglish: Universal laws or permanent state of things are mentioned in Simple Present tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... all the details before she narrated it to the team.
explanationEnglish: When two actions happen in the past, the earlier one is in Past Perfect (had + V3).
subject: English

qEnglish: If they had money, they ..... this house.
explanationEnglish: In second conditional sentences (If + Past Simple), the main clause uses 'would + V1'.
subject: English

qEnglish: It is high time she.............here.
explanationEnglish: Past simple tense follows 'It is high time'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Water ..... at 100 degrees, ice ..... at zero degrees and oil ..... on water.
explanationEnglish: Simple present tense is used to describe universal and scientific facts.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... to school every day. She ..... her homework regularly and never ..... any class.
explanationEnglish: Habitual actions are always represented in Simple Present tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: I ..... you cannot come but I ..... you will ..... us very soon.
explanationEnglish: Stative verbs like 'understand' and 'hope' are usually used in the simple present, not continuous form.
subject: English

qEnglish: Columbus ..... America in 1492, after months at sea.
explanationEnglish: Completed historical events are described using Simple Past tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... the book yesterday, ..... it in one sitting and ..... it to her friend.
explanationEnglish: Actions completed in the past (yesterday) are written in simple past tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: When he ..... young, he ..... every morning and ..... football in the evening.
explanationEnglish: Past habits are described using Simple Past tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... for the company since 2015. She ..... the best employee award twice.
explanationEnglish: Present Perfect Continuous/Perfect is used for durations starting in the past and for life achievements up to now.
subject: English

qEnglish: He ..... in this city since childhood, ..... many schools and ..... a successful doctor.
explanationEnglish: Present Perfect indicates a state or status that started in the past and remains true.
subject: English

qEnglish: They ..... the project yet, but they ..... great progress and ..... all major challenges.
explanationEnglish: Present perfect is often used with 'yet' for actions not finished by now.
subject: English

qEnglish: I was watching TV when the phone ..... and my sister ..... to answer it before me.
explanationEnglish: A continuous past action (was watching) interrupted by sudden past actions (rang, rushed).
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... the best employee, ..... three promotions and ..... the award twice.
optionsEnglish: is working / is getting / is winning
has worked / has received / has won
explanationEnglish: Present perfect is used to indicate accomplishments or status over an unspecified time up to the present.
subject: English

qEnglish: While the children ..... in the park, their mother ..... them and their father ..... pictures.
optionsEnglish: were playing / was watching / was taking
had played / had watched / had taken
have played / have watched / have taken
explanationEnglish: Past continuous is used for multiple actions happening simultaneously in the past.
subject: English

qEnglish: By the time the ambulance ....., the patient ..... consciousness and the doctors ..... treatment.
optionsEnglish: was arriving / was losing / were starting
explanationEnglish: When one past action happens before another, the earlier actions use Past Perfect, and the later one uses Simple Past.
subject: English

qEnglish: When I ..... her last year, she ..... in London for five years and ..... fluent English.
optionsEnglish: have met / has lived / has learned
had met / was living / was speaking
explanationEnglish: Simple past for the meeting; past perfect for the duration that preceded that meeting.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... the letter before the postman ..... and ..... it carefully before sending.
optionsEnglish: was writing / was arriving / was checking
explanationEnglish: The completion of the letter happened before the postman's arrival, requiring Past Perfect for the first action.
subject: English

qEnglish: Look at those clouds! It ..... soon, you ..... an umbrella and you ..... wet without one.
optionsEnglish: is going to rain / will need / will get
shall rain / shall need / shall get
explanationEnglish: 'Be going to' is used for predictions based on present evidence (clouds).
subject: English

qEnglish: This time tomorrow, she ..... her exam, her parents ..... nervously and her teachers ..... for her.
optionsEnglish: will take / will wait / will hope
will be taking / will be waiting / will be hoping
will have taken / will have waited / will have hoped
explanationEnglish: Future continuous is used for actions that will be in progress at a specific time in the future.
subject: English

qEnglish: By next Monday, she ..... the project, ..... it to the committee and ..... their feedback.
optionsEnglish: will finish / will present / will receive
is finishing / is presenting / is receiving
will have finished / will have presented / will have received
explanationEnglish: Future perfect is used for actions that will be completed by a certain point in the future.
subject: English

qEnglish: I ..... you tomorrow, we ..... the plan together and ..... a final decision by evening.
optionsEnglish: am meeting / am discussing / am taking
will meet / will discuss / will take
shall meet / shall discuss / shall take
explanationEnglish: Future simple with 'shall' or 'will' is used for future intentions and plans.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... a doctor when she ..... her degree and ..... her own clinic very soon.
explanationEnglish: In time clauses (when), simple present is used even if the main clause is future.
subject: English

qEnglish: He told me that he ..... tired, ..... the whole night and ..... to leave early.
optionsEnglish: has been / was working / is wanting
explanationEnglish: Sequence of tenses in reported speech: since the reporting verb is past (told), the subordinate verbs shift to past/past perfect.
subject: English

qEnglish: I wish I ..... harder last year, ..... my time wisely and ..... better opportunities.
optionsEnglish: had studied / had used / had grabbed
will study / will use / will grab
explanationEnglish: Wishes about the past are expressed using the Past Perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: It is time we ..... a decision, ..... our differences and ..... forward as a united team.
optionsEnglish: will make / will forget / will move
have made / have forgotten / have moved
explanationEnglish: The expression 'It is time' is followed by the Simple Past tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: She wishes she ..... more confident, ..... better and ..... to speak in public.
optionsEnglish: has been / has performed / has dared
will be / will perform / will dare
explanationEnglish: Wishes about the present are expressed using the Simple Past (subjunctive mood).
subject: English

qEnglish: It is high time he ..... his habits, ..... exercise daily and ..... junk food completely.
optionsEnglish: will change / will do / will avoid
has changed / has done / has avoided
explanationEnglish: 'It is high time' requires the use of Simple Past tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: By 1990, scientists ..... the cure, doctors ..... it on patients and the government ..... it officially.
optionsEnglish: have discovered / have tested / have approved
had discovered / had tested / had approved
were discovering / were testing / were approving
explanationEnglish: Past Perfect is used for actions completed by a specific point in the past.
subject: English

qEnglish: She ..... in Delhi for ten years before she ..... to Mumbai and ..... a new job.
optionsEnglish: was living / had moved / was finding
explanationEnglish: Past perfect indicates a duration that happened before another past event.
subject: English

qEnglish: I ..... this book since morning. I ..... three chapters already and ..... to finish it today.
explanationEnglish: Continuous tense for duration since morning; present perfect for completed units (chapters).
subject: English

qEnglish: Look! The children ..... in the garden, the dog ..... after them and the mother ..... at the window.
optionsEnglish: are playing / is running / is standing
have played / has run / has stood
explanationEnglish: Actions happening right now (Look!) are in the Present Continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: No sooner ..... she ..... the stage than the audience ..... in loud applause.
explanationEnglish: The structure 'No sooner + had + Subject + V3... than' is a standard literary construction.
subject: English

qEnglish: Hardly ..... he ..... the office when the manager ..... him to an urgent meeting.
explanationEnglish: 'Hardly + had + Subject + V3... when' is used for actions happening almost immediately after another.
subject: English

qEnglish: She always _______ an umbrella.
explanationEnglish: The frequency adverb 'always' indicates a habitual action, which requires the simple present tense. 'She' is third-person singular, so 'carries' is used.
subject: English

qEnglish: The driver _______ the taxi to the right on the first turn.
explanationEnglish: Based on the answer key, 'drove' (simple past tense) is chosen to complete the historical or narrative action in the past.
subject: English

qEnglish: The doctor advised me to stop _______.
explanationEnglish: The verb 'stop' is followed by a gerund ('smoking') when it means to discontinue an activity or habit.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ yesterday.
explanationEnglish: The adverb 'yesterday' indicates simple past tense. Negative sentences in simple past use 'did not + base form of verb (V1)'.
subject: English

qEnglish: I am sorry, I _______ anything about your problems.
explanationEnglish: Stative verbs like 'know' are generally not used in continuous forms. The simple past 'didn't know' is appropriate here.
subject: English

qEnglish: "Jyoti _______ the gold medal last year."
explanationEnglish: The time expression 'last year' requires the simple past tense form of the verb, which is 'won'.
subject: English

qEnglish: The company _______ money recently.
explanationEnglish: The keyword 'recently' indicates an action that started in the past and has continuing relevance, perfectly fitting the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: How long you working in this office? Choose the grammatically correct option:
optionsEnglish: How long have you working in this office?
How long have you been working in this office?
How long are working in this office.
How you working long in this office.
explanationEnglish: To ask about the duration of an ongoing action up to the present, 'How long + present perfect continuous interrogative structure' is used.
subject: English

qEnglish: Ajay _______ to Gurgaon last week to visit his parents.
explanationEnglish: The phrase 'last week' refers to a completed past time, requiring the past simple form 'drove'.
subject: English

qEnglish: I didn't want _____ (eat) anything but the cakes looked so good that I couldn't resist _____ (try) one.
explanationEnglish: The verb 'want' is followed by an infinitive ('to eat'), whereas 'resist' is followed by a gerund ('trying').
subject: English

qEnglish: I _____ (write) a letter this morning.
explanationEnglish: According to the official key, 'have written' is preferred here, implying the letter-writing has just concluded within the current day frame.
subject: English

qEnglish: Temperature tomorrow _______ much the same as today.
explanationEnglish: The future time marker 'tomorrow' combined with a state requires the simple future tense construction 'will be'.
subject: English

qEnglish: "I save Rs. 5000 a month. I started in January. So by the end of the year, I _______ Rs. 60,000."
explanationEnglish: The time benchmark 'by the end of the year' indicates an action completed before a future target point, which requires the future perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: We were told that the manager could not see us at that moment, as he _______ some letters to his secretary.
explanationEnglish: The background action happening simultaneously with another past event ('at that moment') requires the past continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: How long _______ waiting for the officer now?
explanationEnglish: The phrase 'How long' along with 'now' marks a continuous action that began in the past and carries on up to now, needing the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: The last train _______ the station at 5:30 am.
explanationEnglish: Fixed public schedules and timetables are consistently spoken or written about using the simple present tense ('leaves').
subject: English

qEnglish: Identify the correct form of tense in the underlined part of the given sentence: When I arrived, Tom _was talking_ on the phone.
explanationEnglish: The construction 'was/were + verb-ing' is the signature structure of the Past Continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Do you also _______ a strange noise coming from the backyard?
explanationEnglish: Interrogative sentences utilizing 'do/does/did' must always use the base form of the verb (V1).
subject: English

qEnglish: Fill in the blank with the correct option: I _______ a play by Pinter.
explanationEnglish: 'I am reading' presents a standard active continuous sentence structure. The others are syntactically invalid or form-broken.
subject: English

qEnglish: He _______ many examples to support his arguments.
explanationEnglish: 'Cite' means to quote or refer to something as evidence or justification; its simple past form is 'cited'.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _____ to call on you yesterday evening, but it rained.
explanationEnglish: 'Was/were going to' describes a past intention or planned event that ultimately did not occur because of a disruption.
subject: English

qEnglish: She is proud that she _____ (achieve) stardom.
explanationEnglish: The present perfect 'has achieved' links a completed action to a current state of being proud.
subject: English

qEnglish: It's time they _______ working on the project.
explanationEnglish: The unreal or presumptive idiom 'It's time + subject' strictly demands a past subjunctive/simple past verb form ('started') to signify an action overdue.
subject: English

qEnglish: The teacher taught us that the earth _______ round.
explanationEnglish: Even inside reported indirect structures controlled by past tense verbs, universal scientific facts stay anchored in the simple present tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Do you (speak) English?
explanationEnglish: An interrogative root query generated via helper verb 'do' requires the primary dictionary base form (V1) 'speak'.
subject: English

qEnglish: He gave the picture to me because he _____ me.
explanationEnglish: To balance the past tense narrative constraint established by 'gave', the clause cause requires a simple past form ('trusted').
subject: English

qEnglish: We suggested (sleep) in hotels but the children were anxious (camp) out.
explanationEnglish: The verb 'suggest' takes a gerund direct object ('sleeping'), while the descriptive phrase 'anxious' takes a full infinitive modifier ('to camp').
subject: English

qEnglish: He is _______ of rich parents.
explanationEnglish: 'Born' is the passive participle adjective form used specifically in context of birth or lineage.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ to Agra before, so I don't wish to go there again.
explanationEnglish: The simple present statement 'I don't wish' implies that the past life experience has present relevance, requiring the present perfect tense 'have been'.
subject: English

qEnglish: "Mahesh told him what happened to his dog, so he _______ home to see how it was."
explanationEnglish: The narrative sequence is established in the past tense ('told', 'happened', 'was'), so the consecutive action 'ran' must also be in the simple past.
subject: English

qEnglish: Prakash _______ tennis for an hour when the news of his selection came in.
explanationEnglish: An action that was ongoing over a period of time up until a specific point in the past ('when the news... came in') takes the past perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Raman saw that the clock _______.
explanationEnglish: The clock had stopped before Raman looked at it. An action completed before a certain point in the past requires the past perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: It is time you _______ (stop) playing.
explanationEnglish: The phrase 'It is time + subject' is followed by a past subjunctive verb form to indicate that something is overdue.
subject: English

qEnglish: She _______ in the hospital since Monday.
explanationEnglish: An action that started in the past and continues up to the present with a time reference ('since Monday') requires the present perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: He _______ (go) to Bombay tomorrow.
explanationEnglish: 'Tomorrow' indicates a future action, which takes the simple future tense 'will go'.
subject: English

qEnglish: He generally _______ (go) for a walk every morning even now.
explanationEnglish: The frequency adverb 'generally' along with 'every morning' highlights a habitual routine requiring the simple present tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: It is time you _______ to bed.
explanationEnglish: The expression 'It is time + subject' strictly requires a simple past/past subjunctive verb form ('went') to suggest an action is overdue.
subject: English

qEnglish: The teacher said that oil _______ (float) on water.
explanationEnglish: Universal truths or scientific facts remain in the simple present tense even inside indirect speech clauses controlled by a past tense verb.
subject: English

qEnglish: The ship _______ (sink) in the ocean yesterday.
explanationEnglish: The time marker 'yesterday' specifies a completed past action, requiring the simple past tense form 'sank'.
subject: English

qEnglish: They _______ (buy) vegetable last week.
explanationEnglish: 'Last week' indicates a definite point in past time, needing the simple past form 'bought'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Mohan _______ rather tired today.
explanationEnglish: 'Seem' is a stative verb linking a state of mind or appearance, and is standardly used in the simple present form 'seems' for singular subjects.
subject: English

qEnglish: She broke down when she _______ the news.
explanationEnglish: Two quick, consecutive actions in the past are connected using the simple past tense ('broke down... when she heard').
subject: English

qEnglish: He kept looking at her, wondering where he _______ her before.
explanationEnglish: An action that occurred before another action in the past ('kept looking') requires the past perfect tense ('had seen').
subject: English

qEnglish: As soon as he _______ he went for a walk.
explanationEnglish: The past simple main clause ('he went') implies that the preceding completed action takes the past perfect tense ('had eaten') or simple past.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ on this project since yesterday.
explanationEnglish: An ongoing action starting from a past point ('since yesterday') up to the present requires the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ him next month.
explanationEnglish: The future adverbial 'next month' paired with first person 'I' takes the simple future construction 'shall visit'.
subject: English

qEnglish: She remarked that she _______ annoyed with him for his lack of sympathy for the victim.
explanationEnglish: The reporting past verb 'remarked' dictates that the subordinate clause should match in the past tense ('felt').
subject: English

qEnglish: They withheld his results. He _______ the proof of his age.
explanationEnglish: The background cause for withholding results in the past requires the past perfect tense ('had not furnished').
subject: English

qEnglish: When my friend came to collect her books, I was a little embarrassed as I _______ only one out of the five books that I had borrowed.
explanationEnglish: An action completed prior to a past reference time point ('came') requires the past perfect tense 'had read'.
subject: English

qEnglish: The passengers in the waiting room are restless after the announcement that the train _______ late.
explanationEnglish: The restlessness of the passengers is a present state ('are'), meaning the train's current status is best expressed in the present continuous 'is running'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Choose the most appropriate option to fill in the blanks: I _______ her _______ a long time, _______ she was a child.
explanationEnglish: 'For' is used for a duration of time ('for a long time') and 'since' marks a specific starting point in time ('since she was a child') with the present perfect 'have known'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Aditi has been working here _______.
explanationEnglish: A length or duration of time ('six months') used with present perfect continuous requires the preposition 'for'.
subject: English

qEnglish: "Is dad in?" "No, he isn't. He _______ home an hour ago. He _______ yet."
explanationEnglish: 'An hour ago' requires the simple past tense ('left'), while the negative adverb 'yet' pairs with the present perfect tense ('has not returned').
subject: English

qEnglish: By tomorrow afternoon, the plane carrying medicines for war victims _______.
explanationEnglish: The time expression 'By tomorrow afternoon' indicates an action completed before a future reference point, requiring the future perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: By the end of March, I _______ doing this job for six years.
explanationEnglish: A continuous action that will be completed by a certain target date in the future takes the future perfect continuous construction 'will have been'.
subject: English

qEnglish: By November, next year she _______ her second book of poems.
explanationEnglish: 'By November, next year' sets a clear future deadline, demanding the future perfect tense form 'will have completed'.
subject: English

qEnglish: I would love to go out with you tomorrow but I _______ dinner with Arvind.
explanationEnglish: The present continuous tense ('am having') is standardly used to express a fixed arrangement or plan for the near future.
subject: English

qEnglish: Vijaya is my best friend. I _______ her for years.
explanationEnglish: Stative verbs like 'know' do not take continuous forms. For a state beginning in the past and continuing up to now with 'for years', the present perfect 'have known' is used.
subject: English

qEnglish: Rita didn't see me wave to her. She _______ in the other direction.
explanationEnglish: An action that was backgrounded or actively ongoing at the specific past time someone else waved takes the past continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: The accident victim _______ by the time they _______ him to the hospital.
explanationEnglish: Based on the official textbook answer key, the state of being dead ('was dead') matches the simple past sequencing with 'rushed'.
subject: English

qEnglish: He used to visit us every week, but he _______ now.
explanationEnglish: The time word 'now' combined with habitual behavior requires simple present tense agreement ('rarely comes').
subject: English

qEnglish: Newton discovered that the force of gravity _______ apples fall.
explanationEnglish: Universal principles, natural laws, or scientific laws remain stated in the simple present tense ('makes').
subject: English

qEnglish: He rested his horse, as it _______.
explanationEnglish: The ongoing reason or background state for resting the horse in the past is represented by the past continuous 'was limping'.
subject: English

qEnglish: He _______ here for the last five years.
explanationEnglish: A time frame indicating duration from a past point up into the present ('for the last five years') demands the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Now that we _______ safe we paused to take a breath.
explanationEnglish: The surrounding clause actions belong entirely inside past timeline conditions ('paused'), meaning the descriptive reason is standard simple past ('felt').
subject: English

qEnglish: You can only alight once the train _______.
explanationEnglish: Time clauses introduced by words like 'once' or 'when' use the simple present tense ('stops') to refer to conditional future completions.
subject: English

qEnglish: The secret of her fitness is that she _______ before sunrise for an hour's speed walk.
explanationEnglish: A lifestyle fact or daily routine is expressed in the simple present tense. 'She' takes 'gets'.
subject: English

qEnglish: You're out of breath. _______?
explanationEnglish: When a past activity has clear, observable physical results in the present ('out of breath'), we use the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ for jobs on jobhunt.com these days with the hope that I might get called for interviews.
explanationEnglish: The time keyword phrase 'these days' identifies a temporary habit or continuous action around the present, fitting the present perfect continuous framework.
subject: English

qEnglish: Jubin _______ the plum cake by this time tomorrow.
explanationEnglish: The specific future limit expression 'by this time tomorrow' requires the future perfect tense ('will have baked').
subject: English

qEnglish: The radio _______ since 10 p.m. yesterday. The neighbours were getting disturbed because of it.
explanationEnglish: An action that was continuously happening over a period up to a point in the past ('were getting disturbed') requires the past perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Identify the 'Tense' of the following sentence - "We have been living here since 1952."
explanationEnglish: The sentence structure 'has/have been + verb-ing' denotes the Present Perfect Continuous Tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Fill up with appropriate Tense - Who _______ her?
explanationEnglish: For a singular interrogative pronoun 'Who' addressing a general fact in the present tense, the negative singular form 'does not know' is appropriate.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _________ (read) the book a number of times.
explanationEnglish: An experience completed multiple times up to the present moment is best expressed using the present perfect tense 'have read'.
subject: English

qEnglish: How often do you _________ your hair?
explanationEnglish: The structure 'How often do you...' represents a question about regular habits, which strictly requires the base form of the verb (V1) 'wash'.
subject: English

qEnglish: My friend _________ (hate) reading but _________ (love) to go to the movies.
explanationEnglish: General preferences and regular likes/dislikes of a singular subject ('My friend') are stated in the simple present tense ('hates, loves').
subject: English

qEnglish: The human body never gets used to noise. The sentence is in:
explanationEnglish: The use of the verb 'gets' with a universal physiological truth positions the sentence in the Present Indefinite (Simple Present) tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: _______ you _______ your homework yet?
explanationEnglish: The adverb 'yet' is standardly paired with a negative or interrogative present perfect construction ('Haven't... done').
subject: English

qEnglish: Yuvika told me that she _______ Paris five years before.
explanationEnglish: In indirect speech, when describing a past action completed prior to the time of reporting ('told'), the past perfect tense ('had visited') is used.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ this necklace. I _______ it for nearly 30 years now.
explanationEnglish: 'Love' is a stative verb typically used in simple present. Possessing an item over a continuous timeframe up to the present ('for nearly 30 years now') takes present perfect 'have had'.
subject: English

qEnglish:
Match the clauses correctly to form complete sentences:
(i) She ______ now.
(ii) I ______ him for a long time.
(iii) I ______ him last five years before.
(iv) He ______ us next week.

Matching elements: (A) had seen, (B) will be meeting, (C) is signing, (D) have known
explanationEnglish: (i) 'now' pairs with present continuous 'is signing'; (ii) 'for a long time' pairs with present perfect 'have known'; (iii) 'five years before' aligns with past perfect 'had seen'; (iv) 'next week' links with future continuous 'will be meeting'.
subject: English

qEnglish: _______ you _______ that book yet? You _______ it for ages.
explanationEnglish: 'Yet' prompts the present perfect 'Haven't finished', and an ongoing activity extending over a duration up to now ('for ages') uses present perfect continuous 'have been reading'.
subject: English

qEnglish: While the guests _______, thieves _______ into the house and _______ a lot of fur coats.
optionsEnglish: had danced, had broken, were stolen
explanationEnglish: A continuous background action in the past ('were dancing') is interrupted or accompanied by quick completed actions in the simple past ('broke', 'stole').
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ my exercise when Hari came to see me last night.
explanationEnglish: An action completed before another past event ('when Hari came') is placed in the past perfect tense ('had done').
subject: English

qEnglish: The sun _______ (rise) in the east.
explanationEnglish: Universal geographical and celestial truths are universally stated in the simple present tense ('rises').
subject: English

qEnglish: Identify the tense in the sentence: "She was dancing so well."
explanationEnglish: The auxiliary 'was' combined with a verb ending in '-ing' forms the past continuous tense structure.
subject: English

qEnglish: Mr. Bhat _______ here since 1990 and has made this place his home.
explanationEnglish: An action that began in the past and continues to the present along with a starting time marker ('since 1990') takes the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Which of the following sentences is in the Present Perfect tense?
optionsEnglish: The team will win the match.
The team has won the match.
The team had won the match.
explanationEnglish: The structure 'has/have + past participle (won)' is the identifier for the Present Perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: The dogs _______ in the street for half an hour.
explanationEnglish: An action continuing over a period up to the present moment, designated by 'for half an hour', requires the present perfect continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Identify the tense used in the following sentence: "I had already completed my work before midnight."
explanationEnglish: The past auxiliary 'had' coupled with the past participle 'completed' forms the Past Perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: She _______ English well and can answer all my questions.
explanationEnglish: The coordinating modal phrase 'can answer' establishes that the person's linguistic capability is a current fact, requiring simple present singular 'knows'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Everybody _______ the rising sun.
explanationEnglish: According to the official book key provided, the historical/narrative simple past option 'worshiped' is selected as correct.
subject: English

qEnglish: Identify the Tense of the following sentence: "She has been teaching for ten years in this school."
explanationEnglish: The verb formula 'has been + V-ing' represents the Present Perfect Continuous tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: Very unwillingly he _______ (turn) down the sound of the TV and _______ (go) to answer the door bell.
explanationEnglish: A sequential pair of past actions requires balanced consistency in the simple past tense ('turned, went').
subject: English

qEnglish: I usually _______ (go) by train, but this weekend I _______ (go) by bus. It _______ (take) longer but it _______ (cost) less.
explanationEnglish: 'Usually' calls for simple present 'go'; a specific exception this week calls for present continuous 'am going'; followed by generic truths 'takes' and 'costs'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Did the Roman slaves _______ the right to own property?
explanationEnglish: Per the official answer key, alternative (D) is specified as the designated choice.
subject: English

qEnglish: He _______ in London for 10 years when I met him last year.
explanationEnglish: An action continuing across a length of time prior to another distinct milestone in the past ('when I met him') requires past perfect continuous 'had been living'.
subject: English

qEnglish: I _______ (write) ten pages since breakfast.
explanationEnglish: A tally of achievements completed from a specific past point up to now ('since breakfast') is structured in the present perfect tense.
subject: English

qEnglish: This telegram has just arrived and the man _______ (wait) in case you _______ (want) to send a reply.
explanationEnglish: An action currently ongoing in the present takes the continuous form 'is waiting', while state verbs like 'want' take the simple present form.
subject: English

qEnglish: When I first met him, he _______ painting.
explanationEnglish: An active continuous activity that was ongoing at the past moment of an initial meeting requires the past continuous tense 'was studying'.
subject: English

qEnglish: You can only alight once the train _______.
explanationEnglish: Subordinate conditional clauses dealing with a necessary future occurrence require the simple present tense form ('stops').
subject: English

qEnglish: He hinted that he _______ some pocket money.
explanationEnglish: The past reporting verb 'hinted' requires the subsequent noun clause to also follow past tense alignment ('wanted').
subject: English
